words.definition: To successfully reach a goal or accomplish something through effort
words.exampleSentence: Many students study abroad hoping to achieve a better understanding of different cultures.

words.definition: As a result; therefore
words.exampleSentence: The company failed to invest in new technology and consequently lost its competitive edge.

words.definition: A person or thing that precipitates an event or change
words.exampleSentence: The printing press acted as a catalyst for the spread of knowledge across Europe.

words.definition: To repeal or do away with a law, right, or formal agreement; to evade a responsibility
words.exampleSentence: The government's attempt to abrogate the treaty was challenged in the International Court of Justice as a violation of customary international law.

words.definition: Large or important enough to be noticed or have an effect
words.exampleSentence: There has been a significant increase in the number of people working from home since 2020.

words.definition: Relating to the natural world and the impact of human activity on it
words.exampleSentence: Environmental concerns have led to stricter regulations on industrial emissions.

words.definition: The process of becoming progressively worse
words.exampleSentence: The rapid deterioration of coral reefs has alarmed marine biologists.

words.definition: An award, privilege, or special honour bestowed as recognition of merit
words.exampleSentence: The research team received the highest scientific accolade for their pioneering work on CRISPR gene-editing technology.

words.definition: The natural world, including the air, water, and land where living things exist
words.exampleSentence: Governments around the world are introducing new policies to protect the environment.

words.definition: A particular attitude toward or way of regarding something
words.exampleSentence: The article examines the issue from the perspective of small business owners.

words.definition: The fact of being widespread or common
words.exampleSentence: The high prevalence of obesity has prompted public health campaigns.

words.definition: To make a formal judgment or decision about a problem or disputed matter
words.exampleSentence: The tribunal was established to adjudicate disputes arising from the bilateral investment treaty between the two nations.

words.definition: A result or effect of an action or situation, often negative
words.exampleSentence: The consequence of deforestation is the loss of biodiversity in many regions.

words.definition: Of considerable importance, size, or worth
words.exampleSentence: The project received a substantial amount of funding from international donors.

words.definition: A system of methods used in a particular area of study
words.exampleSentence: The methodology employed a mixed-methods approach combining surveys and interviews.

words.definition: An increase in power, wealth, or status, often gained in a deliberately self-interested way
words.exampleSentence: The dictator's foreign policy was driven by personal aggrandizement rather than any coherent national interest.

words.definition: To take part or become involved in an activity or event
words.exampleSentence: Students are encouraged to participate in extracurricular activities to develop new skills.

words.definition: The act of obtaining something, such as knowledge, assets, or skills
words.exampleSentence: Language acquisition is most effective when learners are immersed in the target culture.

words.definition: A mutual relationship or connection between two things
words.exampleSentence: The study found a positive correlation between exercise and cognitive function.

words.definition: A thing belonging or appropriate to a period other than that in which it exists, especially one that is conspicuously old-fashioned
words.exampleSentence: The hereditary monarchy, once the cornerstone of governance, is increasingly regarded as a political anachronism in modern democratic societies.

words.definition: Required or essential for a particular purpose
words.exampleSentence: It is necessary for companies to adapt to changing market conditions in order to survive.

words.definition: To publicly recommend or support a particular policy or cause
words.exampleSentence: Many health experts advocate for stricter controls on sugar in processed foods.

words.definition: An inclination or natural tendency to behave in a particular way
words.exampleSentence: Children with a propensity for risk-taking often excel in entrepreneurial activities.

words.definition: A deep-seated feeling of dislike or aversion; a fundamental hostility
words.exampleSentence: The long-standing antipathy between the two academic departments hindered interdisciplinary collaboration for decades.

words.definition: A time or set of circumstances that makes something possible
words.exampleSentence: Studying abroad provides an excellent opportunity to learn about different cultures.

words.definition: To distribute resources or duties for a particular purpose
words.exampleSentence: The government must allocate more funds to public healthcare services.

words.definition: A division between two mutually exclusive things
words.exampleSentence: The false dichotomy between growth and environmental protection has been challenged.

words.definition: The highest point in the development of something; the elevation of someone to divine status
words.exampleSentence: The Renaissance is often regarded as the apotheosis of artistic achievement in Western civilisation.

words.definition: An advantage or positive result gained from something
words.exampleSentence: One benefit of regular exercise is that it improves both physical and mental health.

words.definition: Detailed examination of the elements or structure of something
words.exampleSentence: A thorough analysis of the data revealed several unexpected trends.

words.definition: The combination of ideas to form a coherent whole
words.exampleSentence: The paper offers a synthesis of existing research on memory formation.

words.definition: Approval or praise, especially when formal or official
words.exampleSentence: The proposed curriculum changes received the approbation of the accreditation board after a rigorous review process.

words.definition: To become greater in size, amount, or degree
words.exampleSentence: The number of tourists visiting the city has increased dramatically over the past decade.

words.definition: Roughly; about; used to show that something is almost but not completely accurate
words.exampleSentence: Approximately two-thirds of respondents indicated support for the new policy.

words.definition: An opinion based on incomplete information
words.exampleSentence: Early atomic theory was based on conjecture rather than direct observation.

words.definition: Understood by few; mysterious or secret, especially through specialised knowledge
words.exampleSentence: The arcane rituals of the medieval alchemists, though pseudoscientific, contributed to the development of early chemical laboratory techniques.

words.definition: Systematic investigation to establish facts or reach new conclusions
words.exampleSentence: According to recent research, regular physical activity can reduce the risk of heart disease.

words.definition: A particular feature or part of something that is being considered
words.exampleSentence: One important aspect of the proposal is its potential impact on local employment.

words.definition: The quality of being diverse in character or content
words.exampleSentence: The genetic heterogeneity of the population made it an ideal sample.

words.definition: Showing great care, attention, and persistent effort; diligent
words.exampleSentence: The assiduous documentation of every experimental parameter allowed the researchers to identify the anomalous result's cause.

words.definition: A group of people living in the same place or sharing common interests
words.exampleSentence: The local community came together to organize a fundraising event for the new library.

words.definition: A thing that is accepted as true without proof
words.exampleSentence: The study challenges the assumption that economic growth always leads to higher living standards.

words.definition: The path followed by something or the course of development
words.exampleSentence: The trajectory of carbon emissions suggests we will exceed Paris targets.

words.definition: The patronage, support, or protection of an organisation or individual; favourable conditions
words.exampleSentence: The international conference was conducted under the auspices of the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization.

words.definition: Having the duty to deal with something or having control over someone
words.exampleSentence: Parents are responsible for teaching their children basic manners and values.

words.definition: Resulting in good; favorable or advantageous
words.exampleSentence: Regular physical activity is beneficial for both mental and physical health.

words.definition: A departure from what is normal or expected
words.exampleSentence: The warm winter was considered a statistical aberration rather than a trend.

words.definition: Hostile and aggressive; engaged in a war or conflict
words.exampleSentence: The belligerent rhetoric exchanged between the two nuclear powers raised global concerns about the possibility of armed conflict.

words.definition: Able to be used, obtained, or reached
words.exampleSentence: A wide range of online courses is now available to students around the world.

words.definition: A feature or quality belonging typically to a person, place, or thing
words.exampleSentence: Flexibility is a characteristic of successful organizations in rapidly changing markets.

words.definition: The condition of being responsible for one's actions
words.exampleSentence: Corporate accountability has become a central demand of the environmental movement.

words.definition: The quality of doing good; charitable kindness and generosity
words.exampleSentence: The principle of beneficence in medical ethics requires healthcare providers to act in the best interests of their patients.

words.definition: To help someone or something by giving approval, encouragement, or assistance
words.exampleSentence: Teachers should support students in developing their critical thinking skills.

words.definition: A condition or fact connected with an event or situation
words.exampleSentence: Due to unforeseen circumstances, the conference has been rescheduled to next month.

words.definition: The quality of being open to more than one interpretation
words.exampleSentence: The ambiguity of the treaty clauses led to decades of diplomatic disputes.

words.definition: To cease to resist an opponent or an unwelcome demand; to surrender
words.exampleSentence: The company capitulated to shareholder pressure and agreed to publish an annual sustainability report.

words.definition: Relating to trade, industry, and the management of money
words.exampleSentence: The government introduced new policies to promote economic growth and create jobs.

words.definition: The state of being dedicated to a cause, activity, or relationship
words.exampleSentence: The government's commitment to reducing carbon emissions was outlined in the recent policy document.

words.definition: A comparison between things for explanation or clarification
words.exampleSentence: The professor used an analogy comparing the solar system to an atom.

words.definition: The process of releasing and thereby providing relief from strong or repressed emotions
words.exampleSentence: Aristotle argued that tragedy provides a catharsis of pity and fear, purging the audience of these emotions through vicarious experience.

words.definition: To influence or cause a change in something or someone
words.exampleSentence: Climate change is beginning to affect agricultural production in many parts of the world.

words.definition: Able to be likened to another thing; similar in quality or quantity
words.exampleSentence: The salaries offered in the public sector are not comparable to those in private industry.

words.definition: The technical equipment needed for an activity
words.exampleSentence: The laboratory apparatus included centrifuges and precision scales.

words.definition: The use of trickery or deception to achieve a political, financial, or legal purpose
words.exampleSentence: The financial scandal involved elaborate chicanery, including falsified accounts and offshore shell corporations designed to obscure transactions.

words.definition: To make something smaller or less in amount, size, or degree
words.exampleSentence: Many companies are trying to reduce their carbon footprint by using renewable energy.

words.definition: To pay or reward someone for work, loss, or inconvenience
words.exampleSentence: Workers were compensated for overtime hours at a higher rate.

words.definition: The evaluation of the nature or quality of something
words.exampleSentence: A thorough risk assessment must be conducted before any major project.

words.definition: The use of many words where fewer would do, especially to avoid being clear or direct
words.exampleSentence: The diplomat's circumlocution during the press conference revealed nothing about the government's actual negotiating position.

words.definition: To need something or make something necessary
words.exampleSentence: Most universities require applicants to submit a personal statement along with their grades.

words.definition: To bring something to an end; to reach a judgment by reasoning
words.exampleSentence: The researchers concluded that diet plays a more significant role than exercise in weight management.

words.definition: The state of being intricate or complicated
words.exampleSentence: The complexity of climate systems makes accurate forecasting challenging.

words.definition: Corresponding in size, degree, or proportion; proportionate
words.exampleSentence: The compensation offered was not commensurate with the level of expertise and experience required for the position.

words.definition: Different or diverse types of something
words.exampleSentence: The museum offers various exhibitions throughout the year, attracting visitors of all ages.

words.definition: Acting or done in the same way over time; unchanging
words.exampleSentence: Consistent practice is key to improving language proficiency.

words.definition: The state of conforming to rules or laws
words.exampleSentence: Companies must ensure compliance with environmental regulations.

words.definition: Willing to please others; obliging and agreeable to an excessive degree
words.exampleSentence: The complaisant board of directors approved every managerial proposal without critical scrutiny, failing in their fiduciary duties.

words.definition: To start or create something that is intended to last for a long time
words.exampleSentence: The organization was established in 1995 to help underprivileged children access education.

words.definition: To be a part of a whole; to make up or form something
words.exampleSentence: International students constitute approximately twenty percent of the university's population.

words.definition: The action of making something impure by contact with a harmful substance
words.exampleSentence: The contamination of groundwater by chemicals poses a serious health risk.

words.definition: Naturally accompanying or associated with something; concurrent
words.exampleSentence: The expansion of urban populations brings concomitant challenges for infrastructure, housing, and public service provision.

words.definition: A way of dealing with a situation or problem
words.exampleSentence: The government needs to adopt a new approach to solving the housing crisis.

words.definition: Living or occurring at the same time; current or modern
words.exampleSentence: Contemporary art often challenges traditional notions of beauty and meaning.

words.definition: The quality of being trusted and believed in
words.exampleSentence: The credibility of the study was undermined by undisclosed conflicts of interest.

words.definition: An attitude of patronising superiority; looking down on others
words.exampleSentence: The condescension implicit in the consultant's recommendations alienated the local staff who possessed far deeper contextual knowledge.

words.definition: To make something better or become better
words.exampleSentence: Regular practice is the best way to improve your language skills.

words.definition: A prolonged public disagreement or debate about a matter of opinion
words.exampleSentence: The construction of the new highway has sparked controversy among environmental groups.

words.definition: The process of being damaged or made worse
words.exampleSentence: Soil degradation caused by intensive farming threatens food security.

words.definition: An extensive fire that destroys a great deal of land or property; a large-scale conflict
words.exampleSentence: The political scandal, initially a minor controversy, escalated into a conflagration that consumed the careers of several cabinet ministers.

words.definition: An important topic, problem, or subject for discussion
words.exampleSentence: Environmental issues such as pollution and climate change are widely discussed in the media.

words.definition: A way of behaving that is considered usual or proper in a society or group
words.exampleSentence: Social conventions vary greatly from one culture to another.

words.definition: Written or spoken communication or debate on a topic
words.exampleSentence: Academic discourse on globalization has evolved over the past two decades.

words.definition: Present from birth; having a particular characteristic from birth as though by nature
words.exampleSentence: Congenital heart defects are among the most common birth anomalies, affecting approximately one percent of all live births.

words.definition: A single person considered separately from a group
words.exampleSentence: Each individual has the right to express their own opinion on the matter.

words.definition: Matching or connected with something that has just been mentioned
words.exampleSentence: Sales increased in the first quarter, with a corresponding rise in profits.

words.definition: A difference between things that should be the same
words.exampleSentence: The discrepancy between the data sets suggested a measurement error.

words.definition: Compulsory enlistment for state service, typically military service
words.exampleSentence: The reintroduction of conscription in several European nations reflects heightened concerns about territorial security.

words.definition: A period of ten years
words.exampleSentence: Over the past decade, technology has transformed the way we communicate with each other.

words.definition: Decisive or critical, especially in the success or failure of something
words.exampleSentence: Early childhood education plays a crucial role in cognitive development.

words.definition: The act of spreading information widely
words.exampleSentence: The dissemination of research through open-access journals has increased scientific knowledge.

words.definition: Following as a result or effect; important and significant
words.exampleSentence: The Supreme Court's consequential ruling on privacy rights fundamentally altered the legal landscape for digital surveillance.

words.definition: To give something, especially money, help, or ideas, to achieve a shared goal
words.exampleSentence: Volunteering is a great way to contribute to the local community.

words.definition: To devote time, effort, or resources to a particular task or purpose
words.exampleSentence: The research center is dedicated to finding sustainable solutions for urban transport.

words.definition: The state of being varied or having many forms
words.exampleSentence: Biological diversity is essential for maintaining healthy ecosystems.

words.definition: An association of several companies, organisations, or individuals combining resources for a common purpose
words.exampleSentence: A consortium of major pharmaceutical companies, academic institutions, and governments collaborated to accelerate vaccine development.

words.definition: To roughly calculate or judge the value, amount, or size of something
words.exampleSentence: It is difficult to estimate the total cost of the project at this early stage.

words.definition: Clearly stated, decided, or known; not vague
words.exampleSentence: There is a definite link between smoking and lung cancer, supported by extensive research.

words.definition: The forces that produce change within a system
words.exampleSentence: Understanding group dynamics is essential for effective team management.

words.definition: To violate or go against a law, rule, or agreement; to conflict with
words.exampleSentence: The proposed merger was rejected on the grounds that it would contravene antitrust legislation.

words.definition: Belonging to the present time; happening or being used now
words.exampleSentence: The current education system needs reform to better prepare students for the job market.

words.definition: Laws collectively, or the process of making laws
words.exampleSentence: New legislation on data privacy will come into effect next year.

words.definition: A biological community of interacting organisms and their environment
words.exampleSentence: The mangrove ecosystem provides coastal protection and habitat for species.

words.definition: A confusing and difficult problem or question; a riddle whose answer involves a pun
words.exampleSentence: The ethical conundrum of autonomous vehicle decision-making in unavoidable accident scenarios challenges traditional moral frameworks.

words.definition: A conception or belief about something
words.exampleSentence: There is a common notion that multitasking makes people more productive, but research suggests otherwise.

words.definition: Relating to the structure of populations, including age, income, and education
words.exampleSentence: The marketing campaign was tailored to appeal to a younger demographic.

words.definition: The process of coming into view or becoming apparent
words.exampleSentence: The emergence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria threatens global public health.

words.definition: Evidence or confirmation that supports a statement, theory, or finding
words.exampleSentence: The witness testimony provided independent corroboration of the forensic evidence, strengthening the prosecution's case significantly.

words.definition: All the people living in a particular area, city, or country
words.exampleSentence: The population of the city has grown rapidly due to migration from rural areas.

words.definition: Contingent on or determined by something else
words.exampleSentence: Many developing countries remain heavily dependent on agricultural exports.

words.definition: A thing with distinct and independent existence
words.exampleSentence: The corporation is treated as a legal entity separate from its shareholders.

words.definition: Causing harm or damage; injurious to health or well-being
words.exampleSentence: Long-term exposure to fine particulate matter has deleterious effects on respiratory and cardiovascular health, even at low concentrations.

words.definition: Good, hopeful, or showing improvement
words.exampleSentence: Regular exercise has a positive impact on both mental and physical well-being.

words.definition: Relating to countries with less advanced economies and lower income levels
words.exampleSentence: Microfinance has helped many entrepreneurs in developing countries start small businesses.

words.definition: A state in which opposing forces are balanced
words.exampleSentence: Market equilibrium occurs when supply equals demand.

words.definition: A political leader who seeks support by appealing to popular desires and prejudices rather than rational argument
words.exampleSentence: The demagogue exploited public fears about immigration to consolidate power, undermining democratic norms and institutions in the process.

words.definition: The possibility of developing or achieving something in the future
words.exampleSentence: The young athlete has great potential and could become a world champion.

words.definition: A piece of equipment or machinery designed to serve a specific purpose
words.exampleSentence: Electronic devices such as smartphones and tablets have transformed the way we communicate.

words.definition: Fairness and justice or shares in a company
words.exampleSentence: Intergenerational equity requires considering the impact on future generations.

words.definition: The action of fixing boundaries or limits; a dividing line
words.exampleSentence: The unclear demarcation between federal and state jurisdiction in environmental law creates regulatory complexities for industries operating across state lines.

words.definition: Existing or happening before the present time or before the one just mentioned
words.exampleSentence: The previous year's results were better than what the company achieved this quarter.

words.definition: Intended to be used once and then thrown away
words.exampleSentence: The widespread use of disposable plastic products has created a serious waste management crisis.

words.definition: The gradual destruction or wearing away of something
words.exampleSentence: Coastal erosion has accelerated due to rising sea levels.

words.definition: The final part of a story in which matters are explained or resolved; the outcome of a complex situation
words.exampleSentence: The denouement of the novel reveals that the narrator's account has been unreliable throughout, forcing readers to reconsider their interpretation.

words.definition: A series of actions or steps taken to achieve a particular end
words.exampleSentence: The application process for the scholarship can take several weeks to complete.

words.definition: To have a commanding influence over; to be the most important or conspicuous
words.exampleSentence: Technology companies dominate the stock market, accounting for a large share of total value.

words.definition: Moral principles governing conduct
words.exampleSentence: Research ethics committees review all proposals involving human subjects.

words.definition: To express disapproval of something; to belittle or undervalue
words.exampleSentence: The committee deprecated the practice of awarding contracts without competitive tendering, calling it contrary to principles of transparency.

words.definition: To give something needed or wanted to someone
words.exampleSentence: Schools should provide students with the resources they need to succeed academically.

words.definition: Giving good value for the money spent; sparing in use of resources
words.exampleSentence: Electric cars are more economical to run than traditional petrol vehicles.

words.definition: The process of determining the value or significance of something
words.exampleSentence: A comprehensive evaluation revealed significant improvements in student outcomes.

words.definition: Dried out completely; lacking vitality or spirit through excessive abstraction
words.exampleSentence: The desiccated academic prose of the monograph, stripped of all narrative engagement, made it inaccessible to all but the most dedicated specialists.

words.definition: A place, person, or thing from which something comes or is obtained
words.exampleSentence: Renewable energy sources such as wind and solar power are becoming more affordable.

words.definition: To prepare written material for publication by correcting and revising it
words.exampleSentence: The editor spent hours carefully editing the manuscript before it went to print.

words.definition: The act of preventing someone from being included
words.exampleSentence: Social exclusion can have severe psychological consequences.

words.definition: Light, delicate, and translucent; almost transparent
words.exampleSentence: The diaphanous fabric of the installation piece allowed light to filter through in constantly shifting patterns.

words.definition: The way parts are arranged or organized to form a whole
words.exampleSentence: The essay has a clear structure with an introduction, body paragraphs, and a conclusion.

words.definition: Newly created or becoming prominent
words.exampleSentence: Emerging economies such as India and Brazil are driving global growth.

words.definition: The use of something for profit often unfairly
words.exampleSentence: The exploitation of natural resources has come at the expense of indigenous communities.

words.definition: A forceful and bitter verbal attack against someone or something
words.exampleSentence: The journalist's vitriolic diatribe against the proposed legislation lacked the balanced analysis expected of serious political commentary.

words.definition: Based on customs, beliefs, or practices that have existed for a long time
words.exampleSentence: Traditional teaching methods are gradually being replaced by more interactive approaches.

words.definition: A business or company, or a project or undertaking that involves risk
words.exampleSentence: Small and medium-sized enterprises form the backbone of the national economy.

words.definition: The process of businesses and cultures spreading internationally
words.exampleSentence: Globalization has facilitated the movement of capital across national borders.

words.definition: Intended to teach, instruct, or moralise, often in a way perceived as patronising
words.exampleSentence: The didactic tone of the nineteenth-century novel, with its frequent moral asides, reflects the era's conception of literature as an educational instrument.

words.definition: To keep something in the same condition or at the same level
words.exampleSentence: It is important to maintain a healthy lifestyle by eating well and exercising regularly.

words.definition: To be greater in number or degree than a limit or expectation
words.exampleSentence: Demand for renewable energy has exceeded all previous forecasts.

words.definition: The manner of governing an organization or state
words.exampleSentence: Corporate governance practices have come under scrutiny after accounting scandals.

words.definition: Modest or shy because of a lack of self-confidence; hesitant in asserting oneself
words.exampleSentence: The diffident young researcher hesitated to challenge the eminent professor's interpretation, despite having compelling contradictory evidence.

words.definition: One of the elements or causes that contribute to a result
words.exampleSentence: Stress is considered a major factor in the development of many health problems.

words.definition: To show or display something publicly; to manifest a quality or feeling
words.exampleSentence: The museum will exhibit artefacts from ancient Egyptian civilization.

words.definition: A system of ideas forming the basis of political or economic policy
words.exampleSentence: The ideological divide between parties has widened in recent years.

words.definition: Dissatisfied with those in authority and no longer loyal to them; alienated
words.exampleSentence: The disaffected electorate, disillusioned by broken promises, turned to populist candidates offering radical alternatives.

words.definition: The right or opportunity to use or benefit from something
words.exampleSentence: The internet provides access to a vast amount of information on any topic.

words.definition: Stated clearly and in detail, leaving no room for confusion
words.exampleSentence: The instructions were explicit about the safety procedures to be followed.

words.definition: Protection from disease or exemption from something
words.exampleSentence: Herd immunity provides protection from infectious diseases.

words.definition: To deprive someone of the right to vote or of other rights as a citizen
words.exampleSentence: Historical policies of voter identification have been criticised as attempts to disenfranchise minority communities and low-income citizens.

words.definition: Suitable or acceptable for a particular situation
words.exampleSentence: It is important to wear appropriate clothing for a job interview.

words.definition: The state of being in contact with or subjected to something
words.exampleSentence: Prolonged exposure to air pollution can lead to serious respiratory problems.

words.definition: The process of putting a plan into effect
words.exampleSentence: The implementation of carbon pricing has been slower than anticipated.

words.definition: Not candid or sincere, typically by pretending that one knows less than one really does
words.exampleSentence: The company's claim that the data breach was minor was disingenuous, as internal documents revealed they knew its full extent.

words.definition: The way someone acts or conducts themselves toward others
words.exampleSentence: Children often model their behavior on what they see from adults around them.

words.definition: Belonging to or affecting the outside of something; coming from outside
words.exampleSentence: The university relies on external funding for its research programs.

words.definition: Difference in size or social status between groups
words.exampleSentence: Income inequality has been rising in most developed nations.

words.definition: Essentially different in kind; not allowing comparison
words.exampleSentence: The challenge of integrating disparate data sources from multiple research groups requires standardised metadata protocols.

words.definition: Having the ability or qualities needed to do something
words.exampleSentence: She is a highly capable manager who can handle difficult situations with ease.

words.definition: To make an action or process easier or more likely to happen
words.exampleSentence: Online platforms facilitate communication between students and teachers outside the classroom.

words.definition: Basic physical and organizational structures needed for society
words.exampleSentence: Investment in green infrastructure is essential for sustainable cities.

words.definition: To conceal one's true motives, feelings, or beliefs under a pretence
words.exampleSentence: The politician dissembled skillfully during the interview, expressing concern for the environment while voting against every climate protection measure.

words.definition: The exchange of information or ideas between people
words.exampleSentence: Effective communication is essential for building strong relationships in the workplace.

words.definition: In a way that is appropriate to the particular circumstances
words.exampleSentence: The situation has changed, and we must act accordingly.

words.definition: Reciprocal action or influence between things or people
words.exampleSentence: The interaction between genetic and environmental factors determines disease risk.

words.definition: Cheerful and full of energy; enthusiastically excited
words.exampleSentence: The ebullient mood at the climate summit reflected the historic agreement reached after two decades of stalled negotiations.

words.definition: To examine things in order to find similarities and differences
words.exampleSentence: The essay asks students to compare the education systems in two different countries.

words.definition: About to appear or become available; ready to be given
words.exampleSentence: Details of the new policy will be announced in the forthcoming government report.

words.definition: The explanation of the meaning of something
words.exampleSentence: The interpretation of historical events changes as new evidence emerges.

words.definition: Representing a variety of different Christian traditions; promoting unity among religious groups
words.exampleSentence: The ecumenical dialogue between different faith traditions has contributed to greater mutual understanding and reduced sectarian tensions.

words.definition: Feeling sure about one's own abilities or about something being true
words.exampleSentence: After months of practice, she felt confident giving presentations in English.

words.definition: The basis or groundwork of something; an institution established with an endowment
words.exampleSentence: A strong foundation in mathematics is essential for studying engineering.

words.definition: The act of becoming involved to change an outcome
words.exampleSentence: Early intervention for at-risk children has long-term educational benefits.

words.definition: Vivacious and enthusiastic; giving off bubbles of gas, like a fizzy drink
words.exampleSentence: The effervescent personality of the research director energised the laboratory, fostering a culture of creative scientific inquiry.

words.definition: To eat, drink, or use something up
words.exampleSentence: People in developed countries tend to consume far more resources than necessary.

words.definition: To produce or create something, especially power, ideas, or income
words.exampleSentence: The wind farm is capable of generating enough electricity to power 50,000 homes.

words.definition: The action of showing something to be right or reasonable
words.exampleSentence: The ethical justification for animal testing remains controversial.

words.definition: The ability to produce a desired or intended result; effectiveness
words.exampleSentence: The clinical trial demonstrated the efficacy of the novel therapeutic agent in reducing tumour size among patients with advanced melanoma.

words.definition: Physical harm that impairs the value or function of something
words.exampleSentence: The storm caused extensive damage to buildings along the coast.

words.definition: As a consequence; for this reason
words.exampleSentence: The company failed to innovate and hence lost its market dominance.

words.definition: The great size or importance of something
words.exampleSentence: The magnitude of the crisis overwhelmed international aid organizations.

words.definition: A poem of serious reflection, typically a lament for the dead; a mournful piece of writing
words.exampleSentence: The poet's elegy for the victims of the earthquake captured both personal grief and collective trauma with extraordinary lyrical power.

words.definition: To explain the meaning of a word or concept
words.exampleSentence: The dictionary defines 'sustainability' as the ability to maintain something over time.

words.definition: To emphasize or draw special attention to something
words.exampleSentence: The report highlights the urgent need for investment in public transportation.

words.definition: The movement from one place to another
words.exampleSentence: The migration from rural to urban areas has reshaped global demographics.

words.definition: The omission or slurring of a sound in speech; the process of joining or merging
words.exampleSentence: The deliberate elision of certain historical events from the official narrative raises troubling questions about state-sponsored censorship.

words.definition: The need or desire for a product, service, or resource
words.exampleSentence: There is a growing demand for skilled workers in the technology sector.

words.definition: A proposed explanation made on the basis of limited evidence as a starting point for investigation
words.exampleSentence: The researchers tested their hypothesis by conducting a series of controlled experiments.

words.definition: The action of reducing the severity of something
words.exampleSentence: Climate change mitigation requires substantial emissions reductions.

words.definition: The theory that all knowledge is derived from sense-experience; the practice of relying on observation and experiment
words.exampleSentence: The rise of empiricism in the seventeenth century fundamentally challenged the authority of scholastic philosophy and religious doctrine.

words.definition: A division or section within an organization dealing with a specific area
words.exampleSentence: She works in the marketing department of a large international company.

words.definition: To establish who or what something is; to recognize or pinpoint
words.exampleSentence: Scientists have identified a potential link between air pollution and cognitive decline.

words.definition: Exclusive control of a commodity or service in a market
words.exampleSentence: The company's monopoly attracted antitrust scrutiny.

words.definition: To restrict or burden someone or something in such a way that free action or movement is difficult
words.exampleSentence: The research was encumbered by excessive bureaucratic requirements that consumed time better spent on scientific investigation.

words.definition: To plan and create something with a specific purpose or appearance
words.exampleSentence: Architects must design buildings that are both functional and aesthetically pleasing.

words.definition: So as to give the impression of having a certain quality; apparently
words.exampleSentence: The seemingly simple problem turned out to be quite complex.

words.definition: Discussion aimed at reaching an agreement
words.exampleSentence: Climate negotiations resulted in a landmark agreement to limit emissions.

words.definition: To cause someone to feel drained of energy or vitality; to weaken
words.exampleSentence: The protracted litigation enervated the company's resources, leaving it vulnerable to a hostile takeover bid.

words.definition: Without being affected by; in spite of
words.exampleSentence: Despite the heavy rain, the outdoor concert went ahead as planned.

words.definition: A possible consequence or effect of an action or decision
words.exampleSentence: The study has important implications for how we treat patients with chronic illnesses.

words.definition: The way something is understood or interpreted
words.exampleSentence: Public perception of risk is often influenced by media coverage.

words.definition: A feeling of listlessness and dissatisfaction arising from a lack of occupation or excitement
words.exampleSentence: The existential ennui depicted in twentieth-century literature reflects the spiritual dislocation of modern urban life.

words.definition: To decide or establish something firmly, often after careful thought
words.exampleSentence: The outcome of the experiment will determine whether the hypothesis is correct.

words.definition: To force something unwelcome to be accepted or done
words.exampleSentence: The government plans to impose stricter regulations on plastic waste disposal.

words.definition: Preconceived opinion not based on reason
words.exampleSentence: Unconscious prejudice can affect hiring decisions.

words.definition: Relating to the theory of knowledge, especially with regard to its methods, validity, and scope
words.exampleSentence: The epistemological assumptions underlying quantitative research differ fundamentally from those that inform interpretivist qualitative approaches.

words.definition: To grow or cause something to grow, progress, or become more advanced
words.exampleSentence: The company plans to develop new software to help small businesses manage their finances.

words.definition: Something that motivates or encourages someone to do something
words.exampleSentence: Tax incentives are often used to encourage businesses to invest in renewable energy.

words.definition: Rapid increase in the number of something
words.exampleSentence: The proliferation of mobile devices has transformed communication.

words.definition: Mental calmness, composure, and evenness of temper, especially in difficult situations
words.exampleSentence: The surgeon's remarkable equanimity during the complex 12-hour procedure inspired confidence in the entire operating team.

words.definition: To find something that was previously unknown or hidden
words.exampleSentence: Scientists have discovered a new species of frog in the rainforest of South America.

words.definition: To include or integrate something as part of a whole
words.exampleSentence: The new curriculum incorporates practical skills training alongside theoretical knowledge.

words.definition: A rule or directive made by an authority
words.exampleSentence: Environmental regulations have become more stringent.

words.definition: The use of ambiguous language to conceal the truth or to avoid committing oneself
words.exampleSentence: The witness's equivocation under cross-examination undermined her credibility with the jury.

words.definition: Relating to the home or one's own country
words.exampleSentence: The government aims to boost domestic tourism rather than encouraging travel abroad.

words.definition: Certain to happen; unavoidable
words.exampleSentence: Change is inevitable in any growing organization, and adaptability is key.

words.definition: The capacity to recover quickly from difficulties
words.exampleSentence: Ecosystem resilience depends on biodiversity and species interactions.

words.definition: Extremely delicate and light in a way that seems not of this world; heavenly
words.exampleSentence: The ethereal quality of the dawn light in the mountain landscape has been a recurring subject in Chinese landscape painting for centuries.

words.definition: The process of receiving instruction and acquiring knowledge or skills
words.exampleSentence: Access to quality education is essential for breaking the cycle of poverty.

words.definition: By that means; as a result of that
words.exampleSentence: The company reduced costs, thereby increasing its profit margin.

words.definition: Critical observation or examination
words.exampleSentence: The findings came under intense scrutiny from the scientific community.

words.definition: Using mild or indirect language to describe something unpleasant or harsh
words.exampleSentence: The euphemistic language of the government report described the humanitarian crisis as a 'population displacement event', obscuring its true severity.

words.definition: A change that results when something is done or happens
words.exampleSentence: The new policy had a positive effect on student attendance rates across the school.

words.definition: As is certain to happen; unavoidably
words.exampleSentence: Rapid technological change inevitably leads to job displacement in some sectors.

words.definition: The ability to be maintained over time
words.exampleSentence: Sustainability is a central principle guiding corporate and government policy.

words.definition: To show or declare that someone is not guilty of wrongdoing
words.exampleSentence: The DNA evidence exculpated the defendant after he had spent 15 years wrongfully imprisoned.

words.definition: Working in a well-organized way without wasting time or energy
words.exampleSentence: Modern appliances are more energy-efficient than those produced a decade ago.

words.definition: To combine two or more things to form a whole
words.exampleSentence: The school aims to integrate technology into every aspect of the learning process.

words.definition: The quality of being open and honest
words.exampleSentence: Transparency in government is essential for maintaining public trust.

words.definition: Pressing or demanding; requiring immediate action or attention
words.exampleSentence: The exigent circumstances of the public health crisis warranted emergency measures that would not normally be permissible under existing regulations.

words.definition: To pay someone to work for a company or organization
words.exampleSentence: The factory employs over five hundred workers from the surrounding area.

words.definition: Extreme in degree, strength, or effect
words.exampleSentence: The intense competition for university places puts enormous pressure on students.

words.definition: The process of distributing resources for a purpose
words.exampleSentence: The allocation of funding to renewable energy has increased substantially.

words.definition: Relating to existence; concerned with the nature of human existence as viewed by existentialist philosophy
words.exampleSentence: The existential threat posed by climate change requires a level of international cooperation that has historically proven elusive.

words.definition: To give support, confidence, or hope to someone
words.exampleSentence: Parents should encourage their children to read from an early age.

words.definition: To communicate or be involved with others; to act in a way that affects another
words.exampleSentence: Children learn social skills by interacting with their peers in group activities.

words.definition: A hope or ambition of achieving something
words.exampleSentence: The program aims to raise the aspirations of disadvantaged students.

words.definition: Silly and pointless; devoid of intelligence
words.exampleSentence: The fatuous insistence on maintaining outdated procedures long after their rationale had disappeared frustrated the entire department.

words.definition: The power to be active or do work, often from natural or human sources
words.exampleSentence: Solar panels convert sunlight into electrical energy for heating and lighting.

words.definition: In a permanent, essential, or characteristic way
words.exampleSentence: Some risks are inherently associated with any investment.

words.definition: Something given to make up for a loss or injury
words.exampleSentence: Workers received compensation for health problems from toxic exposure.

words.definition: Lacking initiative or strength of character; irresponsible
words.exampleSentence: The feckless management of the public utility resulted in chronic service failures and massive cost overruns.

words.definition: Very large in size, quantity, or extent
words.exampleSentence: The construction of the new bridge required an enormous amount of time and resources.

words.definition: To take action in order to prevent or alter a result or course of events
words.exampleSentence: The government had to intervene to prevent the collapse of the banking system.

words.definition: A structured set of data stored in computer systems
words.exampleSentence: Researchers compiled a database of ten thousand patient records.

words.definition: Well chosen or suited to the circumstances; pleasing and fortunate
words.exampleSentence: The felicitous phrasing of the treaty's preamble established a constructive tone for the subsequent negotiations.

words.definition: To make certain that something will happen or be the case
words.exampleSentence: Schools must ensure that all students have access to appropriate learning materials.

words.definition: By a strikingly large amount; to a very great degree
words.exampleSentence: Online shopping has grown dramatically over the past decade.

words.definition: Statistical data relating to populations
words.exampleSentence: Changing demographics are reshaping labor markets and welfare systems.

words.definition: Having a red or flushed complexion; excessively elaborate or ornate
words.exampleSentence: The florid prose style of the Victorian novelist, with its elaborate metaphors and extended digressions, fell out of favour in the modernist period.

words.definition: The tools, machines, or items needed for a particular activity
words.exampleSentence: The laboratory is equipped with state-of-the-art equipment for scientific research.

words.definition: To carry out a systematic or formal inquiry to discover and examine facts
words.exampleSentence: The committee was formed to investigate the causes of the industrial accident.

words.definition: The action of making secret information known
words.exampleSentence: Full disclosure of funding sources is required in scientific publications.

words.definition: Patient self-control; restraint and tolerance in the face of provocation
words.exampleSentence: The creditor showed remarkable forbearance by allowing the debtor additional time to repay the loan without imposing penalty fees.

words.definition: Absolutely necessary or extremely important
words.exampleSentence: Clean drinking water is essential for human health and well-being.

words.definition: To cause someone or something to be alone, separate, or apart from others
words.exampleSentence: Patients with contagious diseases are isolated to prevent the spread of infection.

words.definition: The process of gaining control over one's life
words.exampleSentence: Economic empowerment of women improves family health outcomes.

words.definition: Happening by accident or chance rather than design; lucky
words.exampleSentence: The fortuitous discovery of penicillin by Alexander Fleming exemplifies how serendipity can drive scientific progress.

words.definition: To judge or assess the value, quality, or importance of something
words.exampleSentence: Teachers evaluate students' progress through regular tests and assignments.

words.definition: To show or prove to be right or reasonable
words.exampleSentence: The benefits of the project must justify the costs involved.

words.definition: The act of compelling compliance with a law
words.exampleSentence: Enforcement of anti-pollution laws has been inconsistent.

words.definition: Irritable and quarrelsome; difficult to control or manage
words.exampleSentence: The fractious coalition government struggled to pass any meaningful legislation, as internal divisions paralysed decision-making.

words.definition: Information or facts that help prove whether something is true
words.exampleSentence: There is strong evidence that regular exercise reduces the risk of chronic diseases.

words.definition: A classifying phrase or name applied to a person or thing
words.exampleSentence: Food products must display clear labels indicating their nutritional content.

words.definition: The action of spending money or resources
words.exampleSentence: Government expenditure on health has increased over the past decade.

words.definition: Fast and energetic in a rather wild and uncontrolled way
words.exampleSentence: The frenetic pace of trading on the stock exchange during the crisis reflected widespread panic among institutional investors.

words.definition: To look at or study something carefully in order to understand it
words.exampleSentence: Scientists are examining the effects of plastic pollution on marine life.

words.definition: To start or set in motion an activity, product, or organization
words.exampleSentence: The company plans to launch its new smartphone model in March.

words.definition: The state of being easily or conveniently done
words.exampleSentence: A feasibility study assessed the viability of the renewable energy project.

words.definition: Feeding on fruit; fruit-eating
words.exampleSentence: The frugivorous bats play a crucial role in tropical forest ecosystems as seed dispersers for a wide variety of tree species.

words.definition: To be present or real; to have objective reality
words.exampleSentence: Many species of plants and animals exist only in specific climatic conditions.

words.definition: A sheet or thickness of material covering a surface; a level or stratum
words.exampleSentence: The ozone layer protects the Earth from harmful ultraviolet radiation.

words.definition: An irregular rising and falling in number or amount
words.exampleSentence: Price fluctuations in commodity markets destabilize economies.

words.definition: Tending to disappear; fleeting
words.exampleSentence: The fugacious beauty of cherry blossoms, lasting only days, has become a powerful metaphor for impermanence in Japanese aesthetics.

words.definition: To become or make something larger in size, scope, or amount
words.exampleSentence: The company plans to expand its operations into Asian markets next year.

words.definition: Open to new ideas; favoring individual freedom and moderate reform
words.exampleSentence: The country has adopted more liberal immigration policies in recent years.

words.definition: The natural environment where an organism lives
words.exampleSentence: Urban development has destroyed critical habitat for native species.

words.definition: To express vehement protest or strong condemnation; to explode violently
words.exampleSentence: The editorial fulminated against the government's inaction on climate change, calling it a betrayal of future generations.

words.definition: Knowledge or skill gained through direct involvement in an activity
words.exampleSentence: She gained valuable experience while working as an intern at a law firm.

words.definition: In the same way; similarly
words.exampleSentence: The first experiment failed, and the second was likewise unsuccessful.

words.definition: The force that makes something happen
words.exampleSentence: The report provided impetus for reforms in the educational system.

words.definition: Pompous or extravagant in language, style, or manner, especially in a way that is intended to impress
words.exampleSentence: The grandiloquent speeches of the politician contrasted sharply with his party's modest legislative achievements.

words.definition: To make something clear or understandable by describing it
words.exampleSentence: The teacher explained the grammar rules clearly so that everyone could understand.

words.definition: A point beyond which something cannot go
words.exampleSentence: There is a limit to how much information the human brain can process at once.

words.definition: The occurrence or frequency of something undesirable
words.exampleSentence: The incidence of respiratory disease has risen in areas with high pollution.

words.definition: A lengthy and aggressive speech or lecture
words.exampleSentence: The shareholders endured a lengthy harangue from the CEO about the competitive pressures facing the industry.

words.definition: To send goods or services to another country for sale
words.exampleSentence: Germany exports a wide range of automobiles to countries all over the world.

words.definition: To handle or control something skillfully, often in a way that is unfair
words.exampleSentence: Advertisers often manipulate consumer behavior through carefully crafted messages.

words.definition: The process of being separated from others
words.exampleSentence: Social isolation among the elderly is linked to increased mortality risk.

words.definition: Arrogantly superior and disdainful; proud and condescending
words.exampleSentence: The haughty dismissal of alternative approaches by the established researchers discouraged methodological innovation in the field.

words.definition: A distinctive attribute or aspect of something
words.exampleSentence: The new smartphone has several innovative features that appeal to young consumers.

words.definition: The edge or border of something; the difference between cost and selling price
words.exampleSentence: Profit margins have been shrinking due to rising raw material costs.

words.definition: The official power to make legal decisions
words.exampleSentence: The case fell outside the court's jurisdiction.

words.definition: The branch of knowledge that deals with interpretation, especially of biblical or literary texts
words.exampleSentence: Hermeneutics provides a theoretical framework for understanding how meaning is constructed through the interaction between reader and text.

words.definition: The management of money, investments, and other financial resources
words.exampleSentence: A degree in finance can lead to career opportunities in banking and investment.

words.definition: In a general way; without detail; widely
words.exampleSentence: The term 'sustainable development' is broadly used across many disciplines.

words.definition: Legal responsibility for something
words.exampleSentence: The company's liability for environmental damage was established in court.

words.definition: Excessive pride or self-confidence leading to downfall; overweening arrogance
words.exampleSentence: The company's corporate hubris, manifested in reckless expansion and disregard for market signals, eventually led to its spectacular collapse.

words.definition: Able to change or adapt to different circumstances
words.exampleSentence: Many companies now offer flexible working hours to help employees balance work and family life.

words.definition: Fully developed physically or mentally; adult or ripe
words.exampleSentence: The mature students brought valuable work experience to the classroom discussions.

words.definition: A view that is incorrect based on faulty understanding
words.exampleSentence: A common misconception is that all bacteria are harmful.

words.definition: Deserving or causing public disgrace or shame; humiliating
words.exampleSentence: The ignominious defeat of the once-dominant corporation serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of technological complacency.

words.definition: To concentrate attention or effort on something
words.exampleSentence: Students should focus on understanding key concepts rather than memorizing facts.

words.definition: As great, high, or intense as possible or permitted
words.exampleSentence: The maximum temperature recorded during the heatwave was forty degrees Celsius.

words.definition: The ability to move freely
words.exampleSentence: Social mobility has declined with status increasingly determined at birth.

words.definition: An extremely confused, complicated, or embarrassing situation
words.exampleSentence: The diplomatic imbroglio between the three nations arose from conflicting territorial claims to strategically valuable waters.

words.definition: All people born around the same time, typically spanning about 25‚Äď30 years
words.exampleSentence: Younger generations are more concerned about climate change than their grandparents were.

words.definition: A means of communicating information; a middle point between extremes
words.exampleSentence: Television remains a powerful medium for advertising despite the rise of digital platforms.

words.definition: A subtle distinction in meaning or expression
words.exampleSentence: The nuance of the argument was lost in simplified media coverage.

words.definition: A situation in which no progress is possible; a deadlock
words.exampleSentence: The negotiations reached an impasse when neither side was willing to compromise on the core issue of emissions reduction targets.

words.definition: Relating to the whole world; worldwide
words.exampleSentence: Global temperatures have been rising steadily due to increased greenhouse gas emissions.

words.definition: Relating to the mind or the process of thinking
words.exampleSentence: There is growing awareness of the importance of mental health in the workplace.

words.definition: A duty or commitment to do something
words.exampleSentence: Nations have an obligation to assist in climate change adaptation.

words.definition: In accordance with the highest standards; faultless and flawless
words.exampleSentence: The researcher's impeccable attention to experimental protocol ensured that the study's findings were beyond reproach.

words.definition: An aim or desired result that someone works toward achieving
words.exampleSentence: Setting clear goals is the first step toward achieving academic success.

words.definition: Only just; almost not
words.exampleSentence: The government's budget barely covers essential public services.

words.definition: The action of watching and noting phenomena
words.exampleSentence: Careful observation is the foundation of the scientific method.

words.definition: Not showing proper respect; rude or insolent
words.exampleSentence: The journalist's impertinent questions about the minister's personal finances were deflected with practiced ease.

words.definition: The strong effect or influence that something has on a situation
words.exampleSentence: Social media has a significant impact on how young people perceive themselves.

words.definition: In a way that is striking or deserving of attention
words.exampleSentence: Several factors contributed to the decline, most notably the rise in production costs.

words.definition: The process of making something as effective as possible
words.exampleSentence: Supply chain optimization is a key focus for multinational corporations.

words.definition: Unable to be appeased or pacified; relentless and unyielding
words.exampleSentence: The implacable opposition of the pharmaceutical giant to generic competition kept drug prices artificially high for years.

words.definition: To put a plan, decision, or system into effect
words.exampleSentence: The school decided to implement a new curriculum focusing on digital literacy.

words.definition: Concerned with the principles of right and wrong behavior
words.exampleSentence: Companies have a moral responsibility to treat their workers fairly.

words.definition: A measure taken to prevent something dangerous
words.exampleSentence: The precautionary principle guides environmental policy.

words.definition: To dispute or challenge the truth, validity, or integrity of something; to call into question
words.exampleSentence: The defence lawyer impugned the credibility of the prosecution's key witness by highlighting inconsistencies in her prior testimony.

words.definition: To bring goods or services into a country from abroad for sale
words.exampleSentence: Many countries import oil because they lack sufficient domestic reserves.

words.definition: Experienced or done by two or more parties equally; reciprocal
words.exampleSentence: The two countries signed a mutual defense agreement to protect each other's interests.

words.definition: Supreme power or authority within a territory
words.exampleSentence: National sovereignty is challenged by globalization.

words.definition: Just begun and not fully formed or developed; rudimentary
words.exampleSentence: The inchoate theory of plate tectonics, initially dismissed by the geological establishment, eventually revolutionised the earth sciences.

words.definition: Money received regularly for work, investments, or business activities
words.exampleSentence: People with higher incomes tend to have better access to quality healthcare.

words.definition: To a very great extent; extremely
words.exampleSentence: The tourism industry has grown tremendously since the airport was built.

words.definition: The forming of a theory without firm evidence
words.exampleSentence: Financial speculation contributed to the global financial crisis.

words.definition: Intelligently analytical and clear; penetrating and sharp
words.exampleSentence: The critic's incisive analysis of the government's economic policy exposed fundamental flaws that others had overlooked.

words.definition: Not relying on others for support or control; free and self-governing
words.exampleSentence: Learning to be independent is an important part of growing up.

words.definition: Introducing a statement that reverses the previous one
words.exampleSentence: Urban areas often have higher crime rates; conversely, they offer more employment opportunities.

words.definition: The state of being steady and not prone to change
words.exampleSentence: Political stability is necessary for sustained economic development.

words.definition: Persisting tirelessly; never showing signs of fatigue
words.exampleSentence: The indefatigable campaigner for human rights continued her advocacy work well into her eighth decade.

words.definition: To show, point out, or suggest something as a sign
words.exampleSentence: The survey results indicate that most people prefer public transport over driving.

words.definition: A standard or pattern of behavior that is typical or expected
words.exampleSentence: Working from home has become the norm for many office workers since the pandemic.

words.definition: Something that provokes or accelerates an activity
words.exampleSentence: The government introduced a fiscal stimulus package.

words.definition: Not fixed or known in advance; vague and uncertain in extent or nature
words.exampleSentence: The indeterminate nature of quantum measurement challenges classical notions of objective reality.

words.definition: The production of goods or services within an economy
words.exampleSentence: The tourism industry is a major source of revenue for many coastal cities.

words.definition: Until now; until the point in time under discussion
words.exampleSentence: The report reveals information that was hitherto unavailable to the public.

words.definition: A tax imposed on imported or exported goods
words.exampleSentence: The imposition of tariffs sparked a trade war.

words.definition: Wanting to avoid activity or exertion; habitually lazy
words.exampleSentence: The indolent approach of the regulatory agency allowed systemic risks to accumulate unchecked in the financial sector.

words.definition: To give someone facts or knowledge about something
words.exampleSentence: The website informs visitors about the museum's opening hours and exhibition schedules.

words.definition: Great in number; many
words.exampleSentence: Numerous studies have confirmed the link between exercise and improved mental health.

words.definition: Mental strain or a state of latent hostility
words.exampleSentence: Tension between development and environmental protection remains central.

words.definition: Impossible to subdue or defeat; unconquerable
words.exampleSentence: The indomitable spirit of the research team, persisting through repeated funding rejections and experimental failures, eventually led to a breakthrough.

words.definition: Physical harm or damage to a person's body
words.exampleSentence: Regular stretching before exercise can help prevent injury.

words.definition: In a strict manner; rigorously; exclusively
words.exampleSentence: The interview is strictly confidential and will not be shared with anyone.

words.definition: A point at which something begins or changes
words.exampleSentence: When CO2 crosses a threshold, temperature effects become irreversible.

words.definition: Too great or extreme to be expressed or described in words; unspeakable
words.exampleSentence: The ineffable beauty of the night sky from the remote observatory moved even the most cynical members of the research team.

words.definition: A new idea, method, or product that improves upon existing solutions
words.exampleSentence: Technological innovation has transformed the way people communicate and access information.

words.definition: Entirely; completely; to the fullest extent
words.exampleSentence: The committee was not wholly convinced by the arguments presented.

words.definition: A marked change in form or nature
words.exampleSentence: Digital transformation has fundamentally altered the nature of work.

words.definition: Unable to be resisted or avoided; inescapable
words.exampleSentence: The ineluctable advance of technological automation raises profound questions about the future of human employment.

words.definition: An established organization, especially one providing education or public service
words.exampleSentence: Universities are institutions that play a key role in the development of society.

words.definition: Continuing to exist or develop; in progress
words.exampleSentence: There is an ongoing debate about the effectiveness of standardized testing.

words.definition: The state of being unsure about something
words.exampleSentence: Scientific uncertainty should not excuse inaction on climate change.

words.definition: Innocent and unsuspecting; candid and straightforward without guile
words.exampleSentence: The researcher's ingenuous trust in the integrity of the data proved misplaced when the fabrication was uncovered.

words.definition: A tool or device used for a particular purpose, especially in science or music
words.exampleSentence: Learning to play a musical instrument can improve a child's cognitive abilities.

words.definition: A thing that may be chosen; a choice or alternative
words.exampleSentence: Students have the option of studying abroad for one semester as part of their degree.

words.definition: The quality of being logically or factually sound
words.exampleSentence: The validity of findings was confirmed through independent replication.

words.definition: Tending to obstruct or harm; hostile and unfriendly
words.exampleSentence: The regulatory environment was inimical to small business growth, favouring established corporations with substantial compliance resources.

words.definition: The ability to learn, understand, and apply knowledge and skills
words.exampleSentence: Emotional intelligence is just as important as academic ability in the workplace.

words.definition: A part of the body that performs a specific function
words.exampleSentence: The heart is the most vital organ in the human circulatory system.

words.definition: The ability to work successfully or be sustained
words.exampleSentence: The economic viability of renewable energy has improved dramatically.

words.definition: Impossible to understand or interpret; mysterious
words.exampleSentence: The inscrutable expression of the central banker gave no indication of whether interest rates would be adjusted.

words.definition: Involving or relating to more than one country
words.exampleSentence: International students bring diverse perspectives to university campuses.

words.definition: In a literal manner or sense; exactly; used for emphasis
words.exampleSentence: The number of applicants has literally doubled since last year.

words.definition: The state of being exposed to the possibility of harm
words.exampleSentence: The vulnerability of coastal communities to sea levels requires adaptation.

words.definition: The intellectual or highly educated class of a society, especially those interested in culture and politics
words.exampleSentence: The intelligentsia of the early twentieth century played a disproportionate role in shaping modern artistic and political movements.

words.definition: A careful examination or search to discover facts or truth
words.exampleSentence: The police launched an investigation into the cause of the fire.

words.definition: In every case; without exception; always
words.exampleSentence: The bus is invariably late during the morning rush hour.

words.definition: The ability to make good judgments and quick decisions
words.exampleSentence: Business acumen is important for aspiring entrepreneurs.

words.definition: Having or showing a lack of self-discipline; immoderate or excessive
words.exampleSentence: The intemperate language of the political debate alienated moderate voters who might otherwise have supported the policy.

words.definition: To include or affect someone or something as a necessary part
words.exampleSentence: Many science projects involve conducting experiments and analyzing data.

words.definition: In or to a foreign country across the sea
words.exampleSentence: Many students choose to study overseas to gain international experience.

words.definition: The process of becoming more similar
words.exampleSentence: The convergence of digital tech and medicine created new treatments.

words.definition: Unwilling to change one's views or to agree about something; uncompromising
words.exampleSentence: The intransigent position of both parties made any negotiated settlement to the labour dispute impossible.

words.definition: An individual article or unit in a collection or list
words.exampleSentence: Each item on the agenda was discussed thoroughly during the meeting.

words.definition: A formal association between two or more parties for mutual benefit
words.exampleSentence: The university formed a partnership with local businesses to provide internship opportunities.

words.definition: A detailed analysis and assessment of something
words.exampleSentence: Her critique of economic theory sparked academic debate.

words.definition: Fearless and adventurous; courageous in the face of danger
words.exampleSentence: The intrepid explorers documented previously unknown species in the deep-sea trenches, expanding our understanding of extremophile biology.

words.definition: Information and understanding gained through learning or experience
words.exampleSentence: A good teacher not only transmits knowledge but also inspires curiosity in students.

words.definition: Strong and barely controllable emotion or enthusiasm
words.exampleSentence: Her passion for environmental conservation inspired her to study ecology.

words.definition: The process of breaking into smaller parts
words.exampleSentence: Habitat fragmentation threatens the survival of many species.

words.definition: To accustom someone to something unpleasant through prolonged exposure; to harden
words.exampleSentence: Years of working in emergency medicine had inured the doctor to the graphic injuries that would disturb most people.

words.definition: A room or building equipped for scientific experiments and research
words.exampleSentence: Students conduct experiments in the laboratory to test scientific hypotheses.

words.definition: Without human intervention; as a necessary result
words.exampleSentence: The system automatically backs up all files at the end of each day.

words.definition: The quality of being valid or justified
words.exampleSentence: The legitimacy of the election was questioned by observers.

words.definition: Insulting, abusive, or highly critical language
words.exampleSentence: The political debate descended into personal invective rather than substantive discussion of the policy issues.

words.definition: Allowed or required by law
words.exampleSentence: It is legal to drive a car at the age of seventeen in most parts of the UK.

words.definition: To a moderate degree as compared to something else; relatively
words.exampleSentence: The city is comparatively safe when measured against other major urban centers.

words.definition: The act of making partial changes
words.exampleSentence: Genetic modification of crops sparked debate about food safety.

words.definition: Having a particular habit, activity, or interest that is long-established and unlikely to change
words.exampleSentence: The inveterate optimist continued to believe that international cooperation could address the climate crisis, despite decades of political failure.

words.definition: A position on a scale of amount, quality, or degree
words.exampleSentence: Pollution levels in the city have risen significantly over the past few years.

words.definition: A person of the same age, status, or ability as another
words.exampleSentence: Peer pressure can significantly influence teenagers' decisions and behavior.

words.definition: The reason for acting or behaving in a certain way
words.exampleSentence: Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of academic success.

words.definition: Having a tendency to be easily angered; irritable
words.exampleSentence: The irascible professor was renowned for his harsh critiques of student work, though he mentored his doctoral students with unexpected dedication.

words.definition: Probably; very possibly
words.exampleSentence: Students who practice regularly are more likely to perform well on the exam.

words.definition: By a notably large amount or to a notably large extent
words.exampleSentence: The new treatment is considerably more effective than previous options.

words.definition: A thing that blocks one's way or hinders progress
words.exampleSentence: Lack of education remains a major obstacle to economic development.

words.definition: Showing a lack of respect for people or things that are generally taken seriously
words.exampleSentence: The comedian's irreverent treatment of political sacred cows won both acclaim and condemnation from different segments of the audience.
